does: This paper identifies and analyzes three types of variance in circuit discovery for large language models: resampling variance (circuits changing across data batches), rephrasing variance (circuits shifting with prompt reformulations), and sample-wise variance (unfaithfulness fluctuating across individual examples). The authors propose CEAP, an improved circuit discovery method that builds on the existing EAP-IG approach with theoretical guarantees, demonstrating substantial reduction in resampling variance. Through systematic investigation, they show that rephrasing variance stems from different prompt templates activating distinct circuits within the model, revealing fundamental limitations in discovering single unified circuits that comprehensively explain model behavior across varied input formulations.
connects: This work extends mechanistic interpretability research focused on understanding neural network decision-making through circuit analysis. It builds directly on EAP-IG (Edge Attribution Patching with Integrated Gradients), the previous state-of-the-art in circuit discovery, addressing practical limitations discovered through empirical investigation. The paper connects to broader lines of work on neural network interpretability, model faithfulness metrics, and the challenge of finding stable, replicable explanations of model components. It also relates to research on prompt sensitivity and how varying input formulations affect model behavior, contributing to understanding why mechanistic explanations may not generalize across superficially equivalent task formulations.
enables: This work opens directions for more robust mechanistic interpretability by highlighting that circuit discovery variance is not merely a technical artifact but reflects genuine properties of how language models process semantically similar inputs. Future research could develop circuit discovery methods that explicitly account for these variance sources, potentially discovering multiple complementary circuits for single tasks. The findings suggest new investigation into how models learn distributed and redundant representations, and could inform better evaluation frameworks for interpretability research that acknowledge fundamental limits to unified circuit explanations. This also motivates rethinking what comprehensive circuit discovery should aim for, potentially toward ensemble approaches capturing circuit families rather than singular mechanisms.
